titleStmt.title: Letter from P.F. Quinlan to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 23 October 1918
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: P.F. Quinlan

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from P.F. Quinlan to Fr. Nolan regarding the condition of the health of P.F. Quinlan and the confirmation of the death of Fr. Fitzgibbon.
Fr. Thomas Nolan (1867- 1922) became the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus from 1912 to 1922. Besides that, he was a Distribution Committee member responsible for the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who had found refuge in Ireland on account of the World War I.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Personal 68 Middle Abbey Street,
Dublin 23 Oct. 1918
Re Fr Fitzgibbon decd. Dear Fr Nolan
In the above Probate which were so kind
to give me to-do. I have had no further word as
to arrears of pay or bonus. I have written a
line again today informing them that I am
still awaiting further information from them.
I regret to state my clerk has got a bad attack
of the Influenza and is in bed over a week. It
is very inconvenient just now. I have a small
to mind the office when I am in Court. I shall
be in there tomorrow with a couple of ejectment
cases. Mr Maher of Maxwell is dangerously ill
for some time. His brother told me today he was not
expected to recover. I shall let you know any further information I get
and I have all the papers ready for swearing and
lodging.
I shall call and see you the first
chance I get in case I don't see you in
the meantime. Yours very sincerely Frank Quinlan
